title: History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita
subtitle: Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]
caption: Between 1000 and 2018, real gross domestic product per capita in the United Kingdom, Japan, Italy, China, and India remained largely flat for centuries before rising steeply in the modern era. From 1000 through the early 1800s, all five lines hover near the bottom of the vertical axis, staying well below the $5,000 mark. The United Kingdom line begins a sustained upward trajectory around 1800, crossing $10,000 by the mid-19th century and continuing to climb toward $40,000 by 2018. Japan follows a similar path, starting its ascent later in the 19th century and reaching a peak just above $35,000. Italy’s line rises more gradually, ending near $30,000. China and India show minimal growth until the late 20th century, after which both lines turn sharply upward; China reaches approximately $15,000 by 2018, while India ends near $7,000. The most visible pattern is the divergence in growth timing and magnitude: earlier industrializers like the United Kingdom and Japan achieved higher income levels over a longer period, while China and India experienced rapid but more recent increases from a much lower base.
